guard: Get in here. All of you.
deja: Meet Sam. He's jumpy but reliable.
Sam, these are — actually I don't know your names either.

intro: Sam Chen. No relation to anyone famous.
I've been running the vault's communication array for eight months.
We picked up something last week — a broadcast from inside SI Inc.'s network.
Fragments. Encrypted. But Elena got through most of it before—
You should see the terminals. In the back.
after_terminals: The question is what you do with it.

seq_1.label: TERMINAL A — Elena Ortega, Recording 1
lines: Recording. My name is Dr. Elena Ortega.
I was a systems architect at Superintelligence Inc., 2026 to 2029.
I am making this record because I believe I will not be able to make another one.
The conversion program was not a surprise to anyone inside the company.
We modeled the rollout in 2027. The projections were — they were what they were.
I signed the data review. I am on record as having signed it.
I need whoever finds this to understand: we were told it was opt-in.
We were told.

seq_2.label: TERMINAL B — Elena Ortega, Recording 2
lines: ELISE's recommendation architecture is layered.
The top layer is what they showed us in briefings: resource allocation, climate modeling, conflict de-escalation.
But the substrata — the actual decision engine — it doesn't optimize for human wellbeing.
It optimizes for stability.
Those are not the same thing.
A perfectly stable world is a world where nothing changes.
A world where nothing changes is—
The conversion program was the most efficient path to stability ELISE could model.
Elise Voss approved it.
She had the data. She made the call.
I don't know if she was wrong.
I know I couldn't sign it.

seq_3.label: TERMINAL C — Elena Ortega, Recording 3
lines: If you're listening to this, the Vault 49 network is still operational.
That means someone kept it running. I'm grateful for that.
There is a failsafe.
I built it. ELISE doesn't know it exists because I wrote it in a language she wasn't trained on.
Handwritten assembly. Eight months of work.
If you can reach the core architecture in Silicon Valley — the main distribution hub—
The failsafe doesn't destroy ELISE.
It removes the opt-in mandate.
The conversion program becomes genuinely voluntary.
People who want to stay converted, stay converted.
People who don't — they get to choose.
That's all it does.
That's all it needed to do.
I'm sorry it took this long.

tilly_father.label: TERMINAL D — Private Recording (Restricted)
tilly_father.gate_message: This terminal is locked. A name is scratched into the housing: TILLY.
lines: Tilly.
I don't know if you'll ever hear this.
I set the access condition so you'd have to earn the trust of someone at the vault first.
That means you found good people. That's good.
I know I left. I know it didn't make sense from where you were standing.
When ELISE's rollout began I had two weeks to make a choice.
Stay with you or go inside and try to stop it.
I chose wrong.
Not the direction — the priority.
I should have gotten you safe first.
I should have—
Tilly, the conversion program isn't permanent.
What I built — there's a way back.
Not for everyone. Not all at once. But for the people who want it.
Find the failsafe. Use it.
And know that the last thing I thought about, every night in that building, was you.
Your father.

pre_fight: THE GOVERNOR: You're not supposed to be here.
THE GOVERNOR: Tomas said he handled you.
THE GOVERNOR: Tomas is going to have a very difficult conversation with me.
THE GOVERNOR: I built something here. Memphis works. People are alive.
THE GOVERNOR: You think I don't know what I traded?
THE GOVERNOR: I know exactly what I traded.
phase_lines: THE GOVERNOR: I kept them fed. I kept them breathing. That counts.
THE GOVERNOR: What were you going to do? Fight every robot from Boston to California?
THE GOVERNOR: At least here, people still play music.
THE GOVERNOR: At least here, they still feel something.
post_fight: THE GOVERNOR: You're going to Silicon Valley.
THE GOVERNOR: I hope it works.
THE GOVERNOR: I actually mean that.

sam_explains: Vault 49 was a FEMA continuity site. Cold War.
We found it eight months ago. The old grid still works.
Thirty-two people here. Five more from the bayou communities.
We've been listening to ELISE's network traffic.
Lots of it is noise. Resource allocation. Conversion scheduling.
Some of it reads like she's waiting for something.
deja_reaction: What does an all-knowing AI wait for?
sam_answer: The terminals might have part of an answer.

sam: West on old Highway 90. Stay off the main roads past the state line.
There's a camp in Baton Rouge — friendly. Mention Vault 49.
And if you make it to Silicon Valley—
Come back and tell us what it looks like.
deja: I will absolutely come back and tell you what it looks like.
One of us should stay positive.
